perturbation.original_text: XC reasonably determines that its continued involvement with AJR or the Term will result in an adverse impact to XC's reputation.
perturbation.changed_text: XC subjectively determines that its continued involvement with AJR or the Term might result in an adverse impact to XC's reputation.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'reasonably determines' to 'subjectively determines', the contract allows XC to terminate the agreement based on a purely subjective belief, even if there is no objective basis for it. This contradicts the principle of good faith and fair dealing, which generally requires parties to act with honesty and reasonableness in exercising their contractual rights.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied duty of good faith requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance and enforcement of contracts. Changing the standard to 'subjectively determines' allows XC to terminate the agreement based on a mere belief, even without reasonable grounds, potentially violating this duty and making the termination clause unenforceable under Virginia law.

Territorial Applicability and General Rules 6/15/2025 § 8.1A-304 . Obligation of good faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. 1964, c. 219, § 8.1-203; 2003, c. 353 . perturbation.justification: The change from "reasonably determines" to "subjectively determines" allows XC to terminate the agreement based on a mere belief without reasonable grounds. The law explanation states that this potentially violates the implied duty of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing § 8.1A-304 of the Code of Virginia, states that every contract within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. The altered text allows XC to bypass this obligation, creating a legal contradiction as it permits termination based on subjective reasoning, potentially contradicting the requirement of good faith.
